summary:
Hannibal Barca's crossing of the Alps (218 BC) was one of the most audacious strategic marches in military history — the Carthaginian general led approximately 38,000 infantry, 8,000 cavalry, and 37 war elephants from Spain through southern Gaul and across the Alps in autumn, descending into northern Italy to launch the Second Punic War on Roman home territory. The crossing took approximately 15 days through mountain passes (the exact route is still debated by scholars) in deteriorating autumn weather with constant attacks from mountain tribes.

The losses were catastrophic: Hannibal lost perhaps half his army and all but a few elephants to cold, starvation, rockfalls, and ambushes. Yet the strategic logic was sound — by appearing in Italy from a direction Rome had deemed impossible, he gained the element of complete surprise, disrupted Roman plans, and rallied Gallic tribes in northern Italy to his cause. The subsequent Italian campaign produced Hannibal's masterpiece victories at Trebia (218 BC), Lake Trasimene (217 BC), and Cannae (216 BC), which came close to destroying Roman military power. The Alpine crossing became the defining image of bold strategic conception in the Western military tradition, invoked by Napoleon and military strategists through the modern era.
